questions.prompt: Which sentence is a Type 3 (past impossible) conditional?
options: Si hubiera tenido tiempo, te habría llamado.
questions.explanation: Type 3 (past impossible): si + past perfect subjunctive ('hubiera tenido') + conditional perfect ('habría llamado'). The speaker did NOT have time and did NOT call. Option A is Type 1; option B is Type 2; option D is incorrect (conditional after si is never used).

'Embarazada' = pregnant. It is one of the most important false cognates in Spanish. 'Embarrassed' is 'avergonzado/a' or 'apenado/a'. Saying 'estoy embarazada' when you mean embarrassed is a memorable and classic mistake.

questions.prompt: Which pair shows the correct sequence of tenses?
options: Quiero que vinieras / Quería que vengas
Quiero que vengas / Quería que vinieras
Quiero que vinieras / Quería que vinieras
Quiero que vengas / Quería que vengas
questions.explanation: Present main clause → present subjunctive ('vengas'). Past main clause → past subjunctive ('vinieras'). This sequence of tenses rule is fundamental: the tense of the subjunctive mirrors the tense of the main clause.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between 'sino' and 'pero'?
options: 'Sino' corrects or replaces after a negation; 'pero' adds a contrasting comment.
They are interchangeable in all contexts.
'Sino' introduces a consequence; 'pero' introduces a cause.
questions.explanation: 'Sino' corrects/replaces after a negation: 'No es abogado sino médico' (not a lawyer but rather a doctor). 'Pero' adds a contrasting comment: 'Es abogado pero también toca guitarra.' They cannot be swapped — the distinction is structural, not stylistic.

questions.prompt: Which Mexican literary figure appears on the 200-peso note?
options: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz
questions.explanation: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz (1648–1695) appears on the 200-peso note. She was a colonial-era poet and intellectual — one of the first feminist voices in the Americas — and is one of Mexico's most celebrated cultural figures.

questions.prompt: What does 'no me late' express in Mexican Spanish?
options: An instinctive bad feeling — something doesn't feel right.
questions.explanation: 'No me late' (literally 'it doesn't beat to me') is a uniquely Mexican expression for a gut-level negative instinct. It communicates intuitive unease even without a logical explanation — and is taken seriously as a substantive reservation in Mexican culture.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is correct for reporting news in a neutral, journalistic way?
options: Se informó que las autoridades tomaron medidas.
questions.explanation: 'Se informó que' = it was reported that — a formal passive reflexive construction standard in journalism. 'Todo el mundo dice' is informal; 'se rumorea' implies an unverified rumor; 'parece que' is vague. 'Se informó que' signals verified, formal reporting.

questions.prompt: Why is building personal relationships ('las relaciones') important in Mexican business culture?
options: It is required by Mexican commercial law before signing contracts.
Trust is established through personal connection first — business follows relationships, not the other way around.
It helps you learn the technical vocabulary faster.
It is only important when dealing with government clients.
questions.explanation: In Mexican business culture, 'las relaciones' come before transactions. Trust is built through personal connection — coffee, meals, small talk — before getting to business. Skipping this relationship-building phase is perceived as cold or disrespectful and can undermine a business relationship before it starts.
